entry.title: What Is Agentic AI and Why SAP Teams Are Paying Attention in 2026
entry.content:
How Agentic AI Works Inside SAP Environments

The concept of Agentic AI refers to AI systems designed to operate with a defined objective while making structured decisions along the way. Instead of acting as a passive prediction engine, an agent evaluates context, selects actions, executes them, and learns from the outcome. When integrated with enterprise platforms, this model creates a layer of intelligent task execution across business processes. In SAP environments, this idea is gaining attention because enterprise workflows already contain structured data, defined process paths, and measurable outcomes.

The emergence of agentic AI in SAP builds upon the maturity of SAP data ecosystems. Platforms such as SAP S/4HANA, SAP Ariba, and SAP Integrated Business Planning generate high-quality operational data. When AI agents in SAP interact with these systems, they gain the ability to interpret transactional activity and act on it within defined operational boundaries.

For example, intelligent agents can operate across supply chain, finance, and logistics environments where large volumes of decisions are made every day.

Several real-world use cases demonstrate how AI agents interact with SAP workflows and support enterprise decision-making.

1. Autonomous Monitoring of Supply Disruptions

AI agents continuously review procurement signals, vendor commitments, and delivery schedules. When patterns indicate a disruption risk, the system flags the issue and proposes alternative sourcing actions before the disruption reaches operational teams. This early signal analysis helps organizations maintain supply continuity.

2. Transaction Validation Across Financial Workflows

Finance operations involve thousands of approvals, invoices, and transaction records. AI agents in SAP evaluate approval patterns, payment histories, and invoice structures. When irregularities appear, the system can pause a workflow or raise alerts for review. This structured monitoring helps finance teams maintain compliance discipline.

3. Operational Decision Support in Logistics Planning

Logistics operations depend on timely information about shipments, warehouse capacity, and transportation status. With AI for SAP, intelligent agents can interpret delays, route disruptions, or inventory shifts and recommend operational adjustments. These insights allow logistics teams to respond faster to operational signals.

These capabilities introduce a new operational layer for SAP AI teams. Rather than relying only on dashboards and reports, teams can deploy intelligent agents that act as operational co-workers. These agents will be continuously monitoring the system while executing pre-defined actions and escalating more complex operational decisions to human operators.

However, deploying Agentic for SAP Teams requires careful design. SAP environments consist of very mission-critical operations, and an incorrect decision could expose the organization to financial or regulatory risk. Organizations building SAP's AI frameworks will want to ensure that they have established some structured governance, testing protocols, and a defined controlled deployment pipeline prior to implementation.

Because of these complexities, many organizations are exploring specialized agentic AI services that help design, validate, and monitor agent-based architectures within SAP ecosystems.

Why SAP Teams Are Investing in Agentic AI Capabilities

The interest in agentic AI at SAP has increased rapidly because the capabilities of traditional automation have been reached by enterprise teams. Robotics Process Automation (RPA) and scripted workflows work well for processes with a predictable sequence. However, SAP systems are often used within a dynamic environment that is constantly changing. Market volatility, supply chain disruptions, regulatory changes, and inconsistent levels of demand can all result in needing systems to interpret the context of actions and respond appropriately.

Therefore, SAP AI agents provide an opportunity for companies to derive significant operational value. Instead of waiting for human input, intelligent agents interpret signals across multiple datasets and determine the next operational step within defined boundaries.

Several operational factors explain why SAP AI teams are actively evaluating agent-based architectures.

1. Decision Latency in Complex SAP Workflows

Enterprise SAP processes often involve multi-stage approvals and cross-system dependencies. When decisions depend on manual analysis, delays accumulate across operations.

With agentic for SAP teams, intelligent agents can:

  • Scan transactional records across modules
  • Identify decision triggers such as unusual pricing or delayed shipments
  • Initiate follow-up actions or escalation paths

This reduces operational friction while maintaining process oversight.

2. Continuous System Monitoring

Traditional monitoring systems alert teams only when predefined thresholds are crossed. However, SAP environments generate patterns that require contextual interpretation.

By applying AI for SAP, intelligent agents can review system activity continuously and detect early indicators of:

  • Financial inconsistencies
  • Procurement irregularities
  • Inventory allocation imbalances

Because AI agents in SAP evaluate historical patterns alongside real-time data, they can identify emerging risks before operational disruption occurs.

3. Intelligent Process Collaboration

Another reason SAP AI teams are studying agent-based architectures is the ability to coordinate activities across multiple systems. SAP environments often integrate with CRM platforms, logistics software, supplier networks, and external data providers.

Within such ecosystems, agentic AI in SAP can coordinate multiple operational steps:

  • Reviewing procurement demand forecasts
  • Triggering supplier communication workflows
  • Updating inventory planning models

This collaborative automation model enables agentic SAP teams to manage interconnected processes without continuous manual supervision.

4. Enterprise Governance and AI Accountability

Autonomous systems must operate under strict governance structures. SAP environments include compliance obligations, financial regulations, and audit requirements. Organizations, therefore, require transparency in how intelligent agents make decisions.

Well-designed agentic AI services address this concern by introducing:

  • Traceable decision logs
  • Controlled action permissions
  • Validation checkpoints before execution

These mechanisms help SAP AI teams maintain accountability while using AI for SAP in mission-critical environments.

As these operational drivers continue to grow, many enterprises are beginning to treat agent-based architectures as a strategic capability rather than an experimental technology initiative.

Conclusion

Agent-based intelligence represents a significant shift in enterprise automation. Instead of relying only on analytics dashboards or predictive models, organizations can deploy systems that interpret operational signals and take structured action. Within enterprise platforms, agentic AI in SAP allows intelligent agents to monitor workflows, identify decision triggers, and execute tasks under defined governance policies. As AI agents in SAP mature, they are gradually becoming an operational extension of human teams rather than a standalone technology layer.

For organizations exploring Agentic for SAP Teams, successful adoption depends on disciplined system validation, controlled deployment pipelines, and continuous monitoring. This is where specialized quality engineering becomes essential.

entry.title: Why has cut-and-paste behaviour changed? [Rant]
entry.content:
So we know cutting-and-pasting can be a bit of a nightmare in Workzone because by default ctrl-v pastes in any formatting from the source material. This means that unless your editors are well drilled you get inconsistent formatting across your pages that need reformatting. 

But I'm sure many of us knew that ctrl-shift-v pastes without source formatting, so this is a simple workaround that's as easy to use as ctrl-v.

But NOW, following this week's (again unnannouced) release we're seeing a new paste behaviour: When pasting formatted text we're invited to choose from 

  1. keep source formatting
  2. clear formatting
  3. match destination formatting. 

So as a user I now have to select one of these options (none of which dismissed the modal), and if I select 1 or 2 (or just ctrl-shift-v), the content is placed on a new line.

I've counted the interactions. What was previously 1 interaction (ctrl-shift-v) is now somewhere between 3 and 10 interactions depending on your choices and how hard it is to re-combine your sentence. Plus you HAVE to move your hand from keyboard to mouse because you can't dismiss the modal with escape, adding friction to the whole experience

Well done SAP for breaking something as foundational as cutting and pasting. <slow clap>

entry.title: Re: CRRedist.msi error message in iis server
entry.content:
Need more info:

What version of CR for VS are you using?

Where did you get CRRedist.msi from, that's not the typical name for CR for VS runtime or BOE .NET runtime.

Note, you cannot simply copy over the .NET assemblies/dll's, you must use the MSI packages.

Have a read of this blog I created to see what the Project require:

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/upgrading-a-visual-studio-2008-gt-2022-net-project-with-the-latest-cr-net/ba-p/13469215

There is a link in it if using BOE .NET runtime.

ALSO, you MUST be logged in as a local Admin account when installing the MSI to give the MSI package permissions to create Registry Key, Register COM dll's etc.

I’m working on SAP Commerce (B2B Accelerator with Composable Storefront) and on managing replenishment orders (scheduled/repeat orders).

I’ve checked the official documentation, but I’m unable to find clear references on two aspects:

1) Replenishment job configuration

  • Is there a standard cron job already available, or does it need to be created/configured manually?

2) Backoffice management

Once a user creates a replenishment from the frontend (Composable Storefront), for example via “Schedule Replenishment” during checkout:

  • In which section are they displayed (orders, templates, schedules, etc.)?

  • What is the correct object type to search for (e.g. Order, Cart, OrderTemplate, or something else)?

  • Is there a dedicated view, or do they need to be searched manually?

Thanks a lot in advance!

entry.title: Manage and automate BTP certificates using Let's Encrypt
entry.content:
Part II of my BTP Toolbox: This application brings fully automated certificate management to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry. It combines a CAP backend, a Fiori Elements UI, SAP Custom Domain service integration, Cloud Foundry route handling, and Job Scheduler to manage the complete lifecycle of certificates for custom domains using Let’s Encrypt. Instead of handling certificate requests, renewals, uploads, activation, and cleanup manually, teams can operate the whole process through one application with a transparent user interface and scheduled background jobs.

After two years of using this app I decided to make it public on Github as part of my BTP Toolbox. Recently I added some additional features like the Status Flow Chart and the option to manage Scheduler Jobs from the app. 

What It Is Good For

  • Reducing manual effort in certificate operations for SAP BTP custom domains
  • Preventing outages caused by expired certificates
  • Giving operations teams a controlled and visible process instead of script-heavy administration
  • Supporting recurring certificate renewal without relying on manual reminders
  • Managing certificate operations in landscapes where apps and domains are distributed across spaces or regions

The whole certificate process is quite heavy and consists of multiple manual steps documented in SAP Help.

This application automates all tasks on BTP after the initial setup like CNAME mapping (DNS) and registering the top-level-domain.

Highlights

  • End-to-end certificate lifecycle automation using Let's Encrypt ACME challenge
  • Support for creating CSRs, requesting certificates, uploading chains, and activating them
  • Automated renewal of active certificates through scheduled jobs
  • Safe cleanup of expired certificates only when a valid replacement already exists
  • Fiori-based operational UI for certificate actions and scheduler administration
  • Integration with SAP Custom Domain service, Cloud Foundry APIs, Destination service, and Job Scheduler
  • Support for staging mode to test flows safely before consuming production Let’s Encrypt limits
  • Designed to work in SAP BTP Extension Landscapes, with one deployment per region

Github Repository: https://github.com/bkrencker/letsencrypt 

Feel free to use this project, submit pull requests, start discussions about ideas and submit issues on Github (Community Support).

First article in my BTP Toolbox: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/btp-toolbox-app-to-backup-amp-restore-hdi-containers/ba-p/13703879

entry.title: Re: Retention process for customer invoices
entry.content:
Good day @MohanKrishna 

Thank you for the clarification.

The Public Cloud does not offer a standard retention processing feature for customer invoices in AR. Retention Processing is for procurement/supplier invoices, not AR customer invoices.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/d66eb6531de6b64ce10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US

Depending on your business requirements, you can review:

  • Post Customer Down Payment Requests for advance or retained amounts
  • Manage Customer Down Payment Requests
  • Final Billing with Down Payment Settlement to clear earlier down payments against the final invoice
  • Standard AR clearing process.

For your scenario, I recommend you:

  • Check whether you can cover the business requirement through customer down payment requests.
  • If a final invoice is involved, then evaluate Final Billing with Down Payment Settlement
  • Make use of the standard AR clearing process for reconciliation of invoices and payments.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

es ist Zeit für unseren zweiten SAP Stammtisch, diesmal multinational in Deutschland, genauer in Freiburg im Breisgau.

Wann: Donnerstag, 23. April 2026, 18:30 Uhr

Wo: Restaurant "das Quartier", Freiburg im Breisgau, https://www.quartier-freiburg.de/

Unser SAP Stammtisch bietet die Gelegenheit, sich in lockerer Atmosphäre auszutauschen, Erfahrungen zu teilen und gemeinsam über SAP-Themen zu diskutieren. Ob Herausforderungen im Arbeitsalltag, neue Entwicklungen oder Best Practices – hier ist Raum für alle Themen rund um SAP.

Gerne über die Community hier anmelden, dann können wir entsprechend reservieren.

Wir freuen uns auf einen spannenden Austausch und einen gemütlichen Abend mit euch!

entry.title: Re: BAdI to Auto-Populate "Form Header" Text in VA01/VA02
entry.content:
There is NO released, upgrade-safe BAdI in SD that directly allows you to modify standard Sales Order header texts (VBBK/STXH) at save time.

You can :

  1. Create a custom text ID (Form Header)
  2. Maintain Text Procedure
  3. Use VOFM → Text Routin
  4. In routine:

Read:

VBAK-ERNAM

VBAK-ERDAT

VBAK-ERZET

ADRP (via USR21)

Build text dynamicall

5. Assign routine in Access Sequence.

Thank for your help!!

entry.title: Scoping Order in SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 – A Detailed Walk-through
entry.content:
SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 (February 2026) introduces a new enhancement for Maintenance Management: Scoping for Maintenance Orders. This feature helps maintenance planners gather essential on‑site or technical information before finalizing and releasing a maintenance order, reducing rework and improving planning accuracy. In this blog, I will cover: 

  • What a Scoping Order is 
  • How to activate the functionality 
  • Prerequisites 
  • End‑to‑end Scoping Order process 
  • System validations and benefits 

1.      What is a Scoping Order?

During the planning phase of a maintenance order, planners often need additional information, such as: 

  • On‑site conditions 
  • Equipment specifications 
  • Access or environmental constraints 
  • Technical feasibility of planned work 

Previously, such activities were handled informally or by repeatedly changing the main order. 

With Scoping Orders, SAP now provides a structured and auditable way to handle these information‑gathering activities. 

Key Concept 

  • The primary maintenance order becomes the ‘Main Order’
  • A Scoping Order is created as a follow‑on order 
  • The scoping order is used only to collect information and record effort 

The scoping process: 

  • Works with phase‑enabled order types 
  • Also works with non‑phase‑enabled order types 

 

2.      How to Activate Scoping Order Functionality

To activate the ‘Scoping Order’ functionality you must activate the business function ASM_FIELDSCOPING - Scoping for Maintenance Orders. 

When business function is activated, you can see the following changes in a Maintenance Order:

  • New Order Header field ‘Order Role in Scoping’
  • New Operation Execution Stage ‘SCOP’
  • New Processing Context ‘Scoping Order’ (visible in ‘Scoping Order’ only)

Figure 2 – New Order Header Field ‘Order Role in Scoping’'

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Figure 3 – New Operation Execution Stage ‘SCOPE’'

 

 

 

 

 

 

⚠️Important 
Business function ASM_FIELDSCOPING is not reversible. 
Once activated, it cannot be deactivated. 

 3.      Prerequisites

  1. The Scoping Order can only be created using the FIORI app ‘Manage Maintenance Orders (F5241)’. It cannot be created using either SAP GUI transactions (IW31 / IW32) or with Web Dynpro App ‘Process Maintenance Order (W0017)’.
  2. The order type used for Scoping process allows the creation of an order without a notification with screening requirement
  3. The Main Order has not been approved / released
  4. You can't create a scoping order for another scoping order

4.      End-to-End Scoping Process

4.1 Creating a Scoping Order

Steps: 

  1. Open Manage Maintenance Orders (F5241) 
  2. Select the maintenance order requiring scoping 
  3. Open the order in Edit mode 
  4. Ensure the order is in Planning phase 
  5. Add an operation and set Execution Stage = SCOPE 
  6. Save the order 

✅On save, the system automatically creates a Scoping Order in the background. 

Figure 4: Creating Scoping Order

 

 

 

System Behaviour 

  • Main Order receives system status: 
    SCOS (Scoping Outstanding) 
  • Progression to the next maintenance phase is blocked 

Figure 5: Main Order New System Status ‘SCOS’

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Figure 6: Main Order Status Progression Disabled

 

 

 

 

Scoping Order Details 

The scoping order inherits the order type from the main order and the technical object from the order operation. The planning plant, the main work center are copied from the technical object. The default description of the scoping order contains the number of the main order to which it refers.

Figure 7: Scoping Order

 

 

 

 

The Scoping Order attains the ‘Processing Context’ and ‘Order Role in Scoping’ as ‘Scoping Order’.

4.2 Relationship Between the Main Order and the Scoping Order

The relationship between Main Order and Scoping Order can be viewed in the Document flow by clicking the button ‘ Follow-On Orders’

Figure 8: Document Flow - Main Order

 

 

 

Figure 9: Document Flow - Scoping Order

 

 

 

4.3 Approve and Release Scoping Order

After planning the Scoping Order: 

  1. Submit for approval (if applicable) 
  2. Release the Scoping Order 

Result 

  • Main Order status changes to: 
    SCPR (Scoping in Process) 
  • Main Order is still blocked from progressing

Figure 10: Scoping Order Released

 

 

 

 

This result in change in main order system status to SCPR (Scoping in Process). You still can't move the main order to the next phase in the maintenance process.

Figure 11: Main Order System Status changed to SCPR

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

4.4 Dispatch and Execute Scoping Order

You dispatch the scoping order, gather the required information and post a time confirmation for the work performed.

Figure 12: Dispatching Scoping Order using ‘Maintenance Scheduling Board’

 

 

 

Figure 13: Confirming Scoping Order using ‘Perform Maintenance Job’ app

 

 

 

 

 

 

The system status of the main order doesn't change. 

Figure 14: Main Order Status after Confirming Scoping Order – No change

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

4.5 Technically Complete Scoping Order

You change the status of the scoping order to TECO (Technically Completed) to indicate that the scoping tasks are finished.

Figure 15: Scoping Order TECO

 

 

 

 

The system sets the system status SCCO (Scoping Completed) in the main order header. 

Figure 16: Main Order System Status changed to SCCO

 

 

 

 

 

 

You can now continue to process the main order.

Figure 17: Main Order Progression to next phase ‘Submit for Approval’ enabled

 

5.      Validations

  1. Creating Scoping Order from Web Dynpro app ‘Process Maintenance Order (W0017)  Error message issued

Figure 18: Error Message on Creating Scoping Order from Web Dynpro App

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

2. Creating Scoping Order for the Order type with Notification subject to Screening  Error message issued

Figure 19: Error Message on Creating Scoping Order for Order Type with Notification

 

 

 

 

 

 

 6.      Conclusion

The Scoping Order functionality in SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 is a valuable enhancement for organizations that rely on accurate maintenance planning. 

By formalizing site surveys and technical checks into a separate order lifecycle, SAP enables planners to proceed with confidence—only after the required information has been collected and validated. 

If your organization frequently faces uncertainties during planning, Scoping Orders are a feature well worth adopting. 

As scoping order can not be used with Reactive Maintenance (SAP Best Practice solution process 4HH), you may create a separate maintenance order type with no notification link.

7.      Reference:

SAP Help Portal – Scoping for Maintenance Orders

entry.title: PO accruals and Budget consumption
entry.content:
Dear Team,

We have been using SAP ECC (with WBS element and budget AVAC implemented)  and once when a PO accrual is posted in the system, it is consuming the WBS budget which we would like to prevent. In business process, these accruals are reversed in the subsequent month, however business would like to ensure that these PO accruals do not form part of the actual expenses against the WBSe budget.

Current behaviour shows that budget is consumed twice for the same PO. If a PO is created, a commitment is posted and budget is consumed. If a PO accrual is posted for that same PO, budget is taken again. 

Is there a fix recommended by SAP to fix this issue. Any helpful insights are much appreciated

Br, Varun

entry.title: Re: Control Key YBM1 YBM2 S4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Please find below my responses to your questions

1) Why SAP Creates the extra PR line

  • External operation marker: YBM2 isn’t just a label; it tells the system “this operation is performed by a vendor.” The PR line for the operation is the system’s way of ensuring that external work is visible in procurement and controlling, even if the actual service procurement happens through the service line.
  • Integration consistency: In Public Cloud, SAP enforces a strict separation between the operation requirement (the external activity) and the service procurement object. That’s why you see two PR lines — one is structural, one is transactional.

2) What is the use case of the operation PR?

It acts as a framework requirement: a placeholder that ties the maintenance order operation to procurement. It ensures that reporting, analytics, and automation processes can distinguish between internal vs. external work. Even if you don’t create a PO from it, it provides visibility for planners and controllers that “this operation is external.”

3) Why not just use YBM1 + Service Limits?

You can use YBM1 with service limits, and technically procurement will still work. The trade‑off is that you lose the semantic distinction between internal and external operations. That affects:

  • Automation: workflows or integrations that look for external operations (YBM2) won’t trigger.
  • Financial insights: reporting on external vs. internal costs may be blurred.
  • Future extensibility: SAP may deliver innovations (e.g., external workforce management, supplier collaboration) that hinge on YBM2.

The Benefit of YBM2

It’s not about the word “external service”;  it is about system semantics. YBM2 ensures that external work is consistently flagged across procurement, controlling, and analytics. The “extra PR line” is admittedly clutter from a buyer’s perspective, but from SAP’s design view it’s a structural anchor that keeps external operations distinct.

entry.title: To Delete or Not to Delete: No Room for Drama in Identity Provisioning
entry.content:
We naturally find it easier to add than to remove. Adding feels comfortable and safe, while removing feels risky, as if you might break something that’s already working. It’s a well‑known principle in engineering, product design, psychology, and even everyday decision‑making.

Deletion in SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Provisioning (IPS) is no different and may bring the same sense of uneasiness, especially when it involves removing groups and their members. Over the years, IPS has introduced multiple features to control deletion, such as properties, scopes, filters, and conditions. That’s why understanding what they do and which one has priority over the others is essential.

The ABAP Case

Recent improvements in the SAP AS ABAP connector have fine‑tuned the deletion behavior for groups and group members (considered roles and user assignments).

  • If you now delete a group in the source system and that group also exists in the AS ABAP target, running a job will remove the user assignments in the target, not the group itself. Before 27 January 2026, IPS returned an error (“deletion of groups is not supported”) and no deletion occurred.
  • If you now remove a group member in the source system and that assignment exists in AS ABAP, the job will remove the corresponding assignment in the target.
    However, whether IPS also removes pre‑existing assignments (those that IPS did not originally provisioned) depends on the value of the ips.overwrite.existedbefore.assignments property:
    • ips.overwrite.existedbefore.assignments=false
      Only the assignments provisioned by IPS are removed. Pre‑existing assignments are preserved. ABAP keeps what it originally had.
    • ips.overwrite.existedbefore.assignments=true (default)
      The assignments provisioned by IPS are removed along with all pre-existing assignments. ABAP does not keep what it originally had.

     

A Table Says It All

They say a picture is worth a thousand words. In the following deletion scenarios, a table might be worth even more.

Let’s assume you have the following setup:

  • Group 1 exists both in the Identity Directory (source) and in the AS ABAP (target).
  • In the source system, Group 1 is assigned to User 1 and User 2, and these assignments are provisioned to the AS ABAP target by IPS.
  • In the target system, Group 1 is also assigned to User 3, who exists only in AS ABAP and the same user has not been provisioned by IPS from the source.

With this setup in mind, here’s what will happen when you delete the group in the Identity Directory or remove the assignments there, depending on whether the following two properties are enabled or disabled: ips.delete.existedbefore.entities and ips.overwrite.existedbefore.assignments:

 

Best Practices

How to delete users and groups with Identity Provisioning? Here are some best practices to help you decide.

Note: Users and groups are handled differently. Some target systems do not support deleting groups. In those cases, group deletion only results in the removal of its members.

Note: There is no dedicated Deletion job in IPS. Deletion is triggered by a Read job that no longer retrieves a user it previously read from the source system.

When a user can be deleted in the target system:

  • The user was provisioned by IPS from a source to a target system, and the deletion job is run for the same source → target pair.
  • During the deletion job, the user is no longer read from the source (has been deleted in the source or does not match the filter property), or the user no longer meets the condition configured in the source or the target transformation.
  • The deletion job is run in full read mode (ips.delta.read = false), and there are no failed entities in the job statistics.
  • The target transformation does not include skipOperations: ["delete"].
  • No deletion threshold in the target is preventing you from deleting the user, such as a the ips.delete.threshold.users property.
  • The ips.delete.existedbefore.entities=true property is enabled in the target before the deletion job is run.

 ips.delete.existedbefore.entities=true

IPS can delete “existed before” entities in the target, only if this property is enabled before the deletion job is run as mentioned above.

When a user is considered “existed before”:

  • If during the first provisioning job, the user exists in the target and IPS does not create it but only updates it.
  • If the user is created in the target by provisioning job from another source → target system pair, where the target points to the same backend system.
  • If the source or the target system has been reset and IPS provisions the user again.

A common customer scenario where entities are recognized as “existed before” is when they are first read from a source system, provisioned to a target, and then returned to the same source system this time acting as a target (AS ABAP → Identity Directory → AS ABAP). If you are in such a roundtrip scenario and want IPS to delete an entity, enable the property before the deletion job runs.

When a user is not considered “existed before”:

For example, IPS creates a user with provisioning job from Identity Directory →  AS ABAP. Even if the user is later updated by a subsequent provisioning job, if it is deleted in the source, it will be deleted in the target with or without enabling the property.

For more information, see Manage Deleted Entities.

 "scope": "deleteEntity"

{

   "constant":false,

   "targetPath":"$.active",

   "scope":"deleteEntity"

}

Although the scope name suggests deletion, this expression in the write transformations actually triggers an update. It means: When an entity is deleted in the source, don’t delete it in the target, only update it.  In the example above, it sets the user as inactive.

For more information, see Transformation Expressions.

Note: When both ips.delete.existedbefore.entities=true and "scope": "deleteEntity" are configured in the target, IPS processes them in the following order:

  1. IPS first checks the property ips.delete.existedbefore.entities.
  2. IPS then evaluates the scope deleteEntity.
  3. IPS checks the property again to decide the final operation, that is, whether the entity existed before and whether IPS is allowed to delete it.

Resulting behavior:

  • If the property is set correctly and before the job runs, IPS triggers delete.
  • If the property is false, missing, or set only after the job has run, and scope: deleteEntity is present, then the scope is executed, IPS triggers update.

 Filters and conditions

Both filters and conditions can lead to deletion. Filters are always applied on the source system. When set, IPS only reads entities that match the filter criteria. Conditions can be defined on both – the source and the target. When set, IPS reads all entities first and then provisions only those that satisfy the condition.

 
As you work with IPS, use deletion wisely. When configured properly, it’s just as safe and predictable as addition.

entry.title: Re: Birthday and Work Anniversary Cards on the Home Page
entry.content:
Hi @Annelie 

When upgrading the Enable Birthday and Work Anniversary Cards on the Latest Home Page in Upgrade Center, two new scheduled jobs are submitted, one running monthly and another one running daily. 

The system will check on the following standard elements to trigger the reminder cards: 

  • dateOfBirth for birthdays.
  • serviceDate or hireDate for work anniversaries. If both are present, serviceDate takes precedence.

More information is available in the Managing the SAP SuccessFactors User Experience guide, available in the SAP Help Portal https://help.sap.com/docs/successfactors-platform/managing-sap-successfactors-user-experience/enabling-birthday-and-work-anniversary-reminders-on-home-page

Hope this will help, 

Regards, 

Jordi

entry.title: Re: Adding a Column in SNC portal->Release Process->Overview Tab
entry.content:
We've changed requirement like instead of adding column, we just changed the column header name of existing unused column "My Product number" to "APN" and injected the data the data in that column. Now, while downloading data in excel, we're getting "My Product Number" as header name...is there any way to make that to our ""APN" as column header"?"

BADI /SCF/RELEASE_UI code :

  METHOD /scf/if_ex_release_ui~load_overview_tbv.
    TYPES : BEGIN OF ty_matnr,
              matnr TYPE /sapapo/matnr,
            END OF ty_matnr,
            BEGIN OF ty_apn,
              matnr TYPE /sapapo/matnr,
              altpn TYPE /sapapo/mat_apn,
            END OF ty_apn.

    DATA: ls_tbvrowvalues TYPE /scf/stdtbvvalues_str,
          it_matnr        TYPE TABLE OF ty_matnr,
          it_apn          TYPE HASHED TABLE OF ty_apn WITH UNIQUE KEY matnr.

    CONSTANTS : lc_ricefw       TYPE string VALUE 'SNC_REL',
                lc_obj          TYPE string VALUE 'ZSPP_SNC_REL_OVERVIEW',
                lc_en           TYPE c LENGTH 2 VALUE 'EN',
                lc_custmaterial TYPE string VALUE 'CUSTMATERIAL',
                lc_prtmatnr     TYPE c LENGTH 8 VALUE 'PRTMATNR'.

    SELECT SINGLE FROM ztall_enh_switch
      FIELDS active
      WHERE ricefw_id = @LC_ricefw
        AND object_name = @LC_obj
      INTO @DATA(zlv_act).

    IF zlv_act IS NOT INITIAL.
      "Keeping Materials from Import parameter in separate itab for fetching respective EPN from DB.
      it_matnr = VALUE #( BASE it_matnr
                          FOR <lfs_ovw> IN ct_tbvrowdata
                          ( matnr = VALUE #( <lfs_ovw>-rowvalues[ colid = lc_custmaterial ]-cellvalues OPTIONAL ) )
                         ).
      IF it_matnr IS NOT INITIAL.
        SORT it_matnr BY matnr ASCENDING.
        DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES FROM it_matnr COMPARING matnr.

        SELECT FROM /sapapo/matapn AS apn
          INNER JOIN /sapapo/matkey AS matkey ON apn~matid = matkey~matid
          FIELDS matnr, apn~altpn
          FOR ALL ENTRIES IN @IT_matnr
          WHERE matnr = @IT_matnr-matnr AND apn~apntype = @LC_en
          INTO TABLE @IT_apn.

        LOOP AT ct_tbvrowdata ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<lfs_row>).
          "Keep current matnr is one variable.
          DATA(lv_current_matnr) = VALUE #( <lfs_row>-rowvalues[ colid = lc_custmaterial ]-cellvalues OPTIONAL ).
          "Assign Target cell value-"My Product Number" to a field symbol.
          ASSIGN <lfs_row>-rowvalues[ colid = lc_prtmatnr ] TO FIELD-SYMBOL(<lfs_target_cell>).
          "Assign EPN value to that cell.
          <lfs_target_cell>-cellvalues = VALUE #( it_apn[ matnr = lv_current_matnr ]-altpn OPTIONAL ).
        ENDLOOP.
      ENDIF.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.

 BADI WD_BADI_DOMODIFYVIEW code :

  METHOD if_wd_badi_domodifyview~wddomodifyview.
    DATA: lo_view_element TYPE REF TO if_wd_view_element,
          lo_view         TYPE REF TO cl_wdr_view,
          lo_caption      TYPE REF TO cl_wd_caption,
          lo_uielement    TYPE REF TO cl_wd_uielement,
          lo_abs_col      TYPE REF TO cl_wd_abstr_table_column,
          lv_prtmatnr     TYPE string,
          lt_elements     TYPE TABLE OF wdr_element_line. " wdr_element_tab.

    CONSTANTS : lc_ricefw          TYPE string VALUE 'SNC_REL',
                lc_obj             TYPE string VALUE 'ZSPP_SNC_REL_OVERVIEW',
                lc_epn             TYPE string VALUE 'Eng. Part Number(EPN)',
                lc_myproductno     TYPE string VALUE 'My Product No.',
                lc_myproductnumber TYPE string VALUE 'My Product Number'.

    SELECT SINGLE FROM ztall_enh_switch
                  FIELDS active
                  WHERE ricefw_id = @LC_ricefw
                  AND object_name = @LC_obj
                  INTO @DATA(zlv_act).

    IF zlv_act IS NOT INITIAL.
      TRY.
          lo_view ?= view.
          lt_elements = lo_view->get_elements( ).
          LOOP AT lt_elements ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<lfs_element>).
            lo_view_element = view->get_element( id = <lfs_element>-id ).
            TRY.
                "Changing column Header name from "My Product No." to "Eng. Part Number(EPN)".
                lo_uielement ?= lo_view_element.
                lo_uielement->get_tooltip( RECEIVING value = lv_prtmatnr ).
                IF lv_prtmatnr = lc_myproductnumber.
                  lo_uielement->set_tooltip( value = lc_epn ).
                ENDIF.
                CLEAR : lv_prtmatnr.
                "Changing Header Caption from "My Product Number" to "Eng. Part Number(EPN)".
                lo_caption ?= lo_view_element.
                lo_caption->get_text( RECEIVING value = lv_prtmatnr ).
                IF lv_prtmatnr = lc_myproductno.
                  lo_caption->set_text( value = lc_epn ).
                ENDIF.
                CLEAR : lo_view_element, lv_prtmatnr.
              CATCH cx_wd_context.    "To Handle exceptions in
                                      "Shortage Analysis ->Alert Summary & Shortage Analysis ->
                                      "Alert Monitor tab
              CATCH cx_sy_move_cast_error.
                CONTINUE.
            ENDTRY.
          ENDLOOP.
        CATCH cx_sy_move_cast_error.
      ENDTRY.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.

Filter value applied:

entry.title: Re: Disallow Budget Save on Exceed Considering Values on...
entry.content: Standard functionality is to apply the do not exceed the budget on the total budget.  Depending on the number of groups you have, you can create budgets for each of the individual entities based on custom fields and it should not be too cumbersome.  You would have to create an allocation field and a spend field for each entity.  For example, Entity1Allocation would have a formula like,  if (Entity="1", Budget, 0) and the Entity1Spend would have a formula like,  if (Entity="1", merit, 0)

Steps to Reproduce:

  • Used the process hierarchy import template

  • Filled the fields External Reference Name and External Reference ID correctly

  • Uploaded the file successfully (no errors during upload)

Expected Result:
The fields External Reference Name and External Reference ID should be visible in the Proc

Questions:

entry.title: Re: Q&A section to have Public Cloud or Private Cloud option to choose
entry.content:
Thank you for clarifying the current rules of engagement and the role of SAP Managed tags. I fully appreciate the intent;  ensuring that posts carry the right product version and environment context makes it much easier for contributors to provide accurate answers.
At the same time, given the high volume of questions in the forum, it is not practical to ask every poster individually about whether they are on Private Cloud or Public Cloud. This adds overhead for contributors who are already investing significant time in supporting the community.
To make the process more efficient and reduce ambiguity, my suggestion is that the system itself should enforce the selection of the environment (Private Cloud or Public Cloud) before a new post can be created. That way, contributors can focus on solving the issue rather than clarifying the context, and the community benefits from cleaner, more precise discussions.
I remain committed to helping members, but I believe this small improvement would save time for everyone and ensure that posts are consistently tagged with the right environment.

entry.title: Re: Serial Number is locked by User error in MIGO
entry.content:
This is not a functional error but a locking mechanism. If it happens frequently, check with your basis team whether database commit times are unusually long. In some releases, SAP has delivered notes to improve serialization performance and lock handling. Search for OSS notes related to “serial number locked by user” in refurbishment scenarios.

The Best Practice approach is to use the Refurbishment Order flow as intended. Transfer posting → order processing → goods receipt. Avoid running both postings back‑to‑back in parallel sessions. If automation is required, build a small delay or commit check into the custom program or workflow.

Best regards,
Michael

entry.title: Re: PO created via BAPI_PO_CREATE1 cannot be used for GR...
entry.content:
In ME21N, the system automatically determines pricing conditions and sets key fields such as NET_PRICE, PRICE_UNIT, and the GR indicator. In BAPI_PO_CREATE1, if you don’t explicitly pass these fields (or the corresponding X structures), the PO item may be created without a net price or without the GR indicator set. A PO item without a valid net price/condition or without GR relevance will not appear in MIGO for goods receipt.

So to answer your questions

1) Can missing/incomplete pricing cause items to be non‑selectable in MIGO?
Yes. If the item has no net price or is not flagged GR‑relevant, MIGO will not show it.

2) What needs to be populated to finalize pricing like ME21N?
Either pass valid condition records in POCOND/POCONDHEADER, or explicitly set NET_PRICE and PRICE_UNIT in POITEM. ME21N does this automatically via condition technique; the BAPI requires you to supply it.

3) Are there any commonly missed mandatory fields

  • POITEM-GR_IND (Goods Receipt indicator)
  • POITEM-NET_PRICE and POITEM-PRICE_UNIT
  • POITEM-CHANGE_ID (to trigger condition update)
  • Condition type (e.g. PB00) in POCOND

entry.title: Accessibility - Data tables
entry.content:
After an accessibility audit on SAP SuccessFactors, here are some non-compliances that were noted regarding data tables, that we would appreciate to see evolve.

  • Data tables must have a title
  • Column headers must be linked to their cells using `scope=‘col’`
  • Column headers must not be empty

Example:

entry.title: Re: Two different company codes have two different plants...
entry.content:
Purchasing org responsibility is always validated against the plant → company code chain. Even if you assign the purchasing org directly to the plant, the system still checks that the plant belongs to a company code and that the purchasing org is valid for that company code context. If the purchasing org is not explicitly linked to the company code, the consistency check fails, and you see “Purchasing Organization … not responsible for plant …”.

Having said that, you need both assignments. The plant assignment alone is not sufficient, because the system still validates the purchasing org against the company code of the plant.

entry.title: Re: How to verify email delivery for invoices in S/4HANA...
entry.content:
Go to the Manage Billing Documents app (or the relevant output management app). Open the invoice in question. In the Output section, either:

  • Use Resend Output (if the original output request exists), or
  • Create a new output request for the same invoice.

The system will then send the invoice again, this time to the corrected email address.

entry.title: Reversing Steps in Outbound of SAP Logistics Management – Reversal of an LTL Outbound Process
entry.content:
Mistakes happen. Orders get cancelled at the last minute. A warehouse operative picks the wrong item. The item got broken during picking/packing and is not “shippable” anymore. In a traditional warehouse system, unwinding these situations often means a painful sequence of workarounds, manual stock corrections, and support tickets.

SAP Logistics Management takes a different approach: every major step in the outbound process can be reversed or cancelled in a controlled, system-consistent way. This post walks through each reversal point from order to departure — what triggers it, where you do it, and what the system does behind the scenes.

 

The Big Picture: A Chain of Reversals

The outbound process in SAP Logistics Management moves through four phases, and each phase has its own reversal actions:

  1. Outbound Delivery & Logistical Demand — Decline or cancel before warehouse work begins
  2. Transportation Planning — Cancel tendering or subcontracting
  3. Warehouse Activities — Undo picking, packing, staging, or goods issue
  4. Transportation Execution — Reverse carrier arrival, loading, or departure

 

The key principle: work backwards. Each step can only be reversed if the subsequent steps haven’t been completed yet — or if those subsequent steps are reversed first. So let’s go backwards and start with a scenario where the truck departure has already been set and goods issue has already been posted. Let’s reverse everything at the example of an LTL (less than truck load) outbound process:

 

Phase 1: Reversing Transportation Activities

The pickup document tracks the physical handover of freight to the carrier. All three milestones — arrival, loading, departure — can be reversed.

Reverse Carrier Departure

Done in Manage Pickups and Deliveries.

The departure reversal has no prerequisites — it can always be triggered. This handles situations like missing freight data or an incorrect check-out time that needs to be corrected before handover documentation is finalised.

 

Reverse Loading Completion

Done in Manage Pickups and Deliveries.

If freight was loaded incorrectly, loading completion can be reversed, provided the carrier hasn’t departed.

Important for LTL without transportation planning: Consignments are created automatically when loading is confirmed. Reversing loading completion cancels those consignments. When loading is confirmed again, new consignments are created fresh based on the assigned transportation requests.

 

Reverse Carrier Arrival

Done in Manage Pickups and Deliveries.

If a carrier was checked in with incorrect data (wrong time, wrong dock check-in data e.g., like an incorrect license plate number), you can reverse the arrival — as long as loading hasn’t been completed and the carrier hasn’t departed.

 

Phase 2: Reversing Outbound Warehouse Activities

This is where most of the day-to-day reversal scenarios occur — stock discrepancies, damaged goods, last-minute order changes.

Reverse Goods Issue

Done in Manage Outbound Warehouse Activities.

This is the most significant warehouse reversal — undoing the stock movement in S/4HANA. Common triggers include operational errors, last-minute cancellations, or incorrect staging.

The process is asynchronous: when you trigger the reversal, the outbound warehouse request immediately shows Goods Issue Reversal In Progress. The actual reversal happens in SAP S/4HANA:

  • Success: GI status resets to Not Started, lifecycle status returns to Open, and stock and handling units are recreated in SAP Logistics Management.
  • Failure: GI status remains Completed and lifecycle status switches to Completed — indicating the reversal needs to be investigated in S/4HANA.

 

Reverse Staging

Done in Manage Outbound Warehouse Activities.

Staging reversal covers situations where goods have been moved to the staging area but need to go back — for example, damaged items discovered during loading, or a last-minute process change.

The system behaviour differs by shipping mode:

  • LTL process: The reference to the outbound delivery is removed from the pickup document and consignment.
  • Direct LTL process: Packaging information is removed from the pickup document and consignment.

In both cases, the warehouse processing status of the transportation request is reset to Ready, allowing warehouse processing to resume.

Prerequisite: Goods issue must not have been posted and loading must not be marked as completed.

 

Reopen Handling Units and Unpack Items

Done in Packing Work Center.

A packed handling unit can be reopened when items inside are found to be damaged or an order is cancelled post-packing. Reopening moves the HU back to the Open HUs section, unpacked items become available for re-packing, and the packing status on the outbound warehouse request is reset to Open.

Note for parcel processes: If a label has already been printed (which auto-closes the HU), reopening is blocked. You’ll need to reverse staging first.

 

 

Release Stock From an Outbound Warehouse Request

Done in Manage Stock.

Once stock has already been picked (warehouse task completed), you can’t simply cancel the task. Instead, you release the stock from the outbound warehouse request. This effectively undoes the picking assignment without creating a return warehouse task — the stock stays physically where it was picked to, but is no longer tied to the outbound delivery.

Prerequisites: Stock must not be packed in a handling unit and staging must not be completed. If it is, you need to reopen the HU or reverse staging first.

 

Phase 3: Declining the Outbound Delivery or Cancelling the Logistical Demand

Cancel Logistical Demand

Done in Monitor Logistical Demands.

This is the more comprehensive cancellation. When you cancel a logistical demand, the system automatically cascades the cancellation to all follow-on documents: the outbound warehouse request is cancelled and the transportation request is deleted and an integration task is triggered to decline the corresponding outbound delivery in S/4HANA.

Prerequisites: Cancellation without restrictions is possible when processing hasn’t started. Once processing is underway, the following must all be true:

  • No active tendering
  • No active subcontracting
  • No physical warehouse activities started, i.e. no picking warehouse tasks are created or even confirmed
  • Carrier arrival not yet confirmed

If any of these conditions aren’t met, you need to reverse the relevant downstream steps first before you can cancel the logistical demand.

 

This is how the outbound warehouse request looks like:

 

And the outbound delivery in SAP S/4HANA:

 

Summary

The reversal chain in SAP Logistics Management is designed so that each step cascades cleanly — the system tells you what needs to be undone first if prerequisites aren’t met, guiding you through the sequence rather than leaving you to figure it out manually.

 

Want to learn more about SAP Logistics Management?

Explore the SAP Logistics Management Online Course 

Visit the SAP Logistics Management Product Page

Have a look at our Roadmap

Read to online help Reverse and Cancel Steps in the Outbound Process — SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Revaluation of Fixed Asset – Posting in a Separate Document
entry.content:
Currently, the revaluation of assets is posted in the AFAB document during the depreciation run, and the amounts are compressed based on the GL accounts.

Would it be possible to post the revaluation of fixed assets in a separate document and for each asset individually? Can this be customized? This is a legal requirement in Turkey for tax regulations.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Revaluation of Fixed Asset – Posting in a Separate Do...
entry.content:
Currently, your system posts revaluation amounts during the depreciation run (AFAB) in compressed format within the same document, grouping amounts by GL accounts rather than individual assets. This standard behavior consolidates revaluation postings to minimize document volume but reduces asset-level visibility in the GL postings [1],.

The revaluation amounts are calculated and stored at the asset level but posted to GL in summarized form, making it difficult to trace individual asset revaluations directly from the financial documents.

Legal Requirement Assessment

Turkey's tax regulations require individual asset revaluation postings for compliance purposes, necessitating separate documentation for each asset's revaluation amount. This requirement conflicts with SAP's standard compressed posting approach and requires customization to meet local regulatory standards.

The individual posting requirement ensures proper audit trails and supports detailed asset-level reporting required by Turkish tax authorities.

Technical Solution Options Option 1: Configuration Changes

Customization Path: Navigate to SPRO → Financial Accounting → Asset Accounting → Special Valuation → Revaluation of Fixed Assets → Revaluation for the Balance Sheet [1],

Document Splitting Configuration:

  • Configure separate document types for revaluation postings
  • Modify posting rules in transaction OAYR to enable individual asset posting
  • Set up account determination to use different GL accounts for individual revaluations [1],

Posting Logic Adjustments:

  • Enable "Post Revaluation" checkbox in depreciation posting rules
  • Configure revaluation areas to support individual postings
  • Set up separate revaluation offsetting accounts for each asset class [1],

Option 2: Custom Development

Enhancement Points: Utilize BAdI implementations like FAA_DOCLINES_COUNTRY or BADI_FIAA_DOCLINES to customize document line item creation during revaluation posting [2]

Custom Program Requirements:

  • Develop custom posting program to create individual documents per asset
  • Implement logic to bypass standard compression during revaluation posting
  • Create custom transaction type for Turkey-specific revaluation requirements

Although issued for other countries, still the SAP Notes are to be considered: 

SAP Note 3191438 - Romania,Serbia:Revaluation of Fixed Assets: Non-zero balance in case of transferred reserves, Mapping Additional Document Line Items to Fixed Asset Numbers, Incorrect Amounts in Company Code & Global Currencies

SAP Note 3212185 - Revaluation of Fixed Assets for Romania and Serbia: Implementation Leading SAP Note

SAP Note 3410289 - Revaluation of Fixed Assets for Romania and Serbia: Standard Implementation Delivered for C-CEE Add-On

et al.

entry.title: Re: Compatibility Pack: How to check it in your system going forward?
entry.content:
Dear Imran Hasware,
as explained in note 2269324, for SAP S/4HANA systems operated under a cloud subscription contract, the Compatibility Pack use rights for all items will expire Dec 31, 2033. Contract signature date is important here. If valid cloud subscription contracts are in place by end of May, the transition from on premise to our private cloud could be completed even later. For this period of time, you will be allowed to continue using compatibility packages in your on premise system.

This means, you can still use compatibility package functionalities  in your SAP S/4HANA 1909 system until completion of the transition to SAP Private Cloud in July.
Best regards,
Stefan

entry.title: Get text via SAP-Scripting from type GuiShell Subtype HTMLViewer
entry.content:
I automate SAP with VBA and SAP-Scripting-Interface and I actually need your help, please

When a business partner is opened, SAP displays warnings in a separate bar, e.g., "Please clarify address." I would like to extract this text, but it doesn't seem to be embedded in the element's structure like it would be in a popup. This bar isn't present when there are no warnings, and when there is a warning, it consists of two child elements.

wnd[0]/shellcont[0]/shellcont/shell/shellcont[0]/shell --> The button to hide the entire bar
wnd[0]/shellcont[0]/shellcont/shell/shellcont[1]/shell --> The area containing the text

Some properties of the element containing the text:
BrowserHandle: Nothing
Children.Count: 0
Handle: 788884
ID: wnd[0]/shellcont[0]/shellcont/shell/shellcont[1]/shell
SubType: HTMLViewer
Text: SAP.HTMLControl.1
Type: GuiShell

What I have found so far in my research always leads to the solution of reading the InnerText of the document's body via the BrowserHandle, just as Google's AI does, for example

            Dim shellObj, browserObj, doc, text
         
            Set shellObj = session.FindById("wnd[0]/shellcont[0]/shellcont/shell/shellcont[1]/shell")
            Set browserObj = shellObj.BrowserHandle
            Set doc = browserObj.Document
            text = doc.body.innertext

That won't work for me because the BrowserHandle is set to Nothing.
For your information, since many posts I found mentioned problems with the BrowserHandle in SAP 770, I'm working with SAP 800, patch level 14.

Does anyone have any idea how I can access the text that is displayed in this element?

entry.title: Re: MM01 Real time check Classification
entry.content: You need to move the check into a material master save exit/BAdI that can read classification after it Is entered. BADI_MATERIAL_CHECK alone won’t work because classification isn’t yet committed at that point. So you can try with EXIT_SAPLMGMU_001 or BAdI_MATERIAL_MASTER to enforce the rule at save time. Also, in your code, try calling FM CLAF_CLASSIFICATION_OF_OBJECTS with the material number and class type (usually 001 for material). If no classification entries are found, issue an error (MESSAGE e...) to prevent save.

entry.title: Re: Calculating Manager input in the comment section for CDP
entry.content:
@William_Yu1I am not able to add a image here of the data model but I will do my best to explain the structure.

Job Information > Primary Goal Fields / Legal Entity / Primary Goal Fields / Form > Competency / Form Section - Section Comment, Section ID and Section Name selected.

Here is a further breakdown of the formula created to capture if the comment has been left for the section or no:

IF( [Performance Management#Form Section#Section ID] =2, IF(ISNULL([Performance Management#Form Section#Section Comment]), 0, 1), 0 )

Formula to capture if the comment has been left by Manager or Employee, as we don`t have separate field for manager comment in CDP.

IF( [SECTION_3] = 1, IF( ISNULL([Form Section#user_information_sac#Basic User Information#Section Comment Owner User ID]), 0, IF( [Form Section#user_information_sac#Basic User Information#Section Comment Owner User ID] = [User#Basic User Information#Employee ID], 0, 1 ) ), 0 )

Capturing Sum of Manager Comments: IF(ISNULL([M_C]), 0, [M_C]) + IF(ISNULL([M_C_1]), 0, [M_C_1]) + IF(ISNULL([M_C_2]), 0, [M_C_2]) + IF(ISNULL([M_C_3]), 0, [M_C_3]).

The story is a table format at the moment and starts with Manager Name, Employee ID, Employee Name, Current Route Step Name, Current Role, Manager_Comment_My Current Role, Areas of Strength, Action Plan Goal, Manager_Comment_Area of Strength, etc.,

Do you have any best practice how to capture and calculate Manager Input in CPD?

entry.title: How to Fix Groovy Script Warnings and Upgrade to Script Version 2.x in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
What Changed in the March 15, 2026 Release

SAP introduced enhanced design guideline checks for Groovy scripts in integration flows.

These checks help developers ensure their scripts:

  • Avoid unsupported libraries

  • Avoid wildcard imports
  • Remove duplicate or unused imports
  • Use supported runtime classes
  • Remain compatible with Groovy runtime 4.0.29

The script editor now categorizes issues into:

  • Errors
  • Warnings
  • Information

A new Upgrade Readiness Check highlights script incompatibilities that could break in future runtime upgrades.

Unlike some design guideline checks, Groovy script compatibility issues cannot be skipped, meaning they must be addressed.

What I Noticed After the Update

After the March 15 release update, I reviewed one of my integration flows and noticed that the Groovy script used in my Exception Subprocess was showing warnings in the script editor.

The script itself was still working correctly and running smoothly, and there were no runtime errors in the integration flow. However, the editor flagged some upgrade readiness warnings, mainly related to wildcard imports and compatibility with future runtime upgrades.

Once I upgraded the script to Groovy Script Version 2.x, the editor automatically resolved the issues and removed the warnings, making the script fully compliant with the latest SAP design guidelines.

Example Warning: Wildcard Import

One of the warnings highlighted was:

Usage of Wildcard (*) imports on Internal Classes/Packages is not supportedand may be removed in future.

The script contained the following import:

 
import groovy.json.*;

Wildcard imports like this are now discouraged because they can introduce hidden dependencies and compatibility issues.

Fixing the Wildcard Import Issue

The solution was simple: replace the wildcard import with a specific class import.

Old Import

 

import groovy.json.*;

Fixed Import

import groovy.json.JsonBuilder

After replacing the import, the script complied with the new design guidelines.

Fix Script Incompatibilities Using the Script Editor

SAP has made it easier to fix issues directly inside the Groovy script editor.

Step 1 — Open the Groovy Script

Navigate to your integration flow and open the local Groovy script step.

Step 2 — Check the Problems View

The script editor automatically detects issues and displays them in the Problems tab.

Two major categories are shown:

Upgrade Readiness Check

Checks for:

  • Unsupported libraries
  • Deprecated classes
  • Wildcard imports
  • Duplicate imports

General Script Check

Checks general scripting practices.

Step 3 — Apply Automatic Fix

For issues that support automatic fixes:

  1. Select the issue
  2. Click Fix
  3. Confirm the suggested changes

The editor automatically updates the code.

Step 4 — Save and Deploy

After resolving the issues:

  1. Save the script
  2. Return to the integration flow
  3. Simulate or deploy the flow

Upgrading Groovy Script from Version 1.x to 2.x

Another useful feature is the direct upgrade option for Groovy scripts.

Why Upgrade to Groovy Script 2.x?

Upgrading provides several benefits:

  • Compatibility with Groovy runtime 4.0.29
  • Automatic fixes for incompatible code
  • Access to modern Groovy language features
  • Better support for future runtime upgrades

How to Upgrade the Script

  1. Open the Groovy script in the editor
  2. Click Upgrade
  3. Confirm the upgrade

All integration flow steps using this script will automatically move to Groovy Script Version 2.x.

SAP also keeps a backup of the previous script version in the Scripts folder in case you want to revert.

Real Example: Exception Subprocess Script Upgrade

In my case, the Exception Subprocess script was originally written using Groovy Script 1.x.

Old Script (Groovy Script 1.x)

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;import groovy.util.XmlSlurper;import java.util.HashMap;import groovy.json.*;import org.apache.camel.Exchange;import org.apache.camel.builder.SimpleBuilder;import groovy.xml.*import groovy.util.*;

This script included:

  • Wildcard imports
  • Older SAP integration libraries

Updated Script (Groovy Script 2.x)

After clicking Upgrade, the script was automatically updated by the editor.

 
// This is Groovy Script Version 2.x, running with Groovy runtime 4.import com.sap.it.script.v2.api.Message;import com.sap.it.script.v2.api.language.SimpleBuilder;import groovy.json.JsonBuilder

Key improvements:

  • Uses the new SAP script API
  • Removes wildcard imports
  • Compatible with Groovy runtime 4

The functionality of the script remained the same, but the code is now future-ready and compliant with SAP standards.

Recommendation

After upgrading your scripts:

  • Always simulate the integration flow
  • Verify the message processing logs
  • Ensure the output matches expected behavior

Even though SAP provides automatic fixes, validation before production deployment is recommended.

Conclusion

The March 15, 2026 SAP Integration Suite update introduced helpful improvements for maintaining clean and upgrade-ready integration scripts.

Using the Upgrade Readiness Check and the Groovy Script 2.x upgrade option, developers can easily fix incompatibilities and align their scripts with SAP best practices.

In my case, upgrading the script and fixing the wildcard import warning resolved the issues highlighted by the editor and ensured my Exception Subprocess script is ready for future SAP runtime upgrades.

entry.title: RAP Unmanaged Draft: Early Numbering and Associations for Unique IDs
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION

In RAP, Implementing numbering mechanisms during these operations is essential to ensure unique identifiers for newly created entities.
This blog is exposure the best for numbering during Creation and Creation by Association, in Unmanaged Scenario with Draft. How to handle number creation with Draft Scenario's.

Some Information about Numbering
Basically, Numbering is the concept to process assigning unique values to the key fields of business entities during Create and Create By Association Operations.

Unmanaged Early Numbering:

  • Developers need to implement custom code logic to create and assign keys values during the creation and create by association operation. 
  • %is_draft – It is a component of the transactional key (%tky) in RAP. 
    • It tells the framework whether to operate on the draft table or the active persistent table for a given operation. 
    • %is_draft = mk-on reads from draft table 
    • %is_draft = mk-off reads from active table 

Problem Statement

A sales clerk begins creating a Sales Order in Fiori. The order header is saved as a draft, but items need to be added immediately under that draft. To ensure consistency, the system must generate a unique Sales Order ID right away and assign incremental item numbers without waiting for activation.

Importance:

  • Early numbering guarantees unique identification across drafts and active records.

  • Associations ensure header–item linkage is preserved.

  • Draft handling allows users to save incomplete transactions safely and activate later without losing data integrity.

Behavior Definition of Root Entity

early numbering → enables key generation during root creation.

unmanaged implementation in class zbp_i_vd_head unique;
strict ( 2 );
with draft;

define behavior for ZI_VD_HEAD alias Header
draft table zdft_vd_head
lock master
total etag CreatedOn
authorization master ( instance )
early numbering // Early numbering declaration in root entity
{
  create;
  update;
  delete;
  association _item { create; }
  field ( readonly : update ) CustNo;

  draft action Edit;
  draft action Activate optimized;
  draft action Resume;
  draft action Discard;
  draft determine action Prepare;
}
mapping for zvd_t_hsale {
  CustNo     = cust_no;
  SalesOrder = sales_order;
  CreatedOn  = created_on;
  EntryTime  = entry_time;
  Name       = name;
  DocCat     = doc_cat;
}


Behavior Definition of Item Entity

early numbering → enables key generation during item creation.

define behavior for ZI_VD_ITEM alias Item
draft table zdft_vd_item
lock dependent by _head
authorization dependent by _head
early numbering // Early numbering declaration in item entity
{
  update;
  delete;
  field ( readonly ) CustNo;
  association _head;

  mapping for zvd_t_isale {
    CustNo     = cust_no;
    SalesOrder = sales_order;
    DocItem    = doc_item;
    MatNumber  = mat_number;
    MatGroup   = mat_group;
    CreatedOn  = created_on;
  }
}

Behavior Pool Implementation

This method ensures that every new entity gets a unique identifier (CustNo) during creation if it wasn’t already provided.

UUID Generation:

  • cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_x16_static( ) generates a globally unique key.

  • This avoids collisions and guarantees uniqueness across drafts and active records.

Mapped Root:

  • %cid → correlates client-side temporary IDs with backend entities.

  • %key → holds the generated or existing key.

  • %is_draft → ensures draft state is preserved.

  • CustNo → the actual business key stored in the entity.

METHOD earlynumbering_create.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(entity) WHERE CustNo IS INITIAL.
    DATA(ls_uuid) = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_x16_static( ).

    APPEND VALUE #( %cid      = entity-%cid
                    %key      = VALUE #( CustNo = ls_uuid )
                    %is_draft = entity-%is_draft
                    CustNo    = ls_uuid ) TO mapped-header.
  ENDLOOP.

  LOOP AT entities INTO entity WHERE CustNo IS NOT INITIAL.
    APPEND VALUE #( %cid      = entity-%cid
                    %key      = entity-%key
                    %is_draft = entity-%is_draft ) TO mapped-header.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

Mapped Item:

This method handles early numbering when creating items via association (CREATE-BY-ASSOCIATION).

  • First, it retrieves the maximum cust_no from the database table zvd_t_isale for the current entity.

  • Then, it checks existing items in the helper class buffer (gt_isale) to ensure numbering continuity.

  • For each new item (DocItem IS INITIAL), it increments the maximum by 10 and assigns the new number.

  • %cid → client-side correlation ID.

  • %key → generated key with CustNo.

  • %is_draft → preserves draft state.

  • CustNo → ensures linkage to the root entity.

METHOD earlynumbering_cba_item.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(entity).
    SELECT MAX( cust_no ) FROM zvd_t_isale
      INTO (lv_max)
      WHERE cust_no = -CustNo.

    LOOP AT zcl_vd_helper=>get_instance( )->gt_isale INTO DATA(item)
      WHERE cust_no = entity-%key-CustNo.
      IF item-doc_item > lv_max.
        lv_max = item-doc_item.
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.

    LOOP AT entity-%target INTO DATA(t_item) WHERE DocItem IS INITIAL.
      lv_max += 10.

      APPEND VALUE #( %cid      = t_item-%cid
                      %key      = VALUE #( CustNo = entity-%key-CustNo )
                      %is_draft = t_item-%is_draft
                      CustNo    = entity-%key-CustNo ) TO mapped-item.
    ENDLOOP.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

  • Item inherits lock and authorization from the header.

  • Early numbering ensures item keys are generated consistently.

  • Association links items back to the header BO.

Persistence Methods

  • Create Method
METHOD create.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(entity).
    DATA(header) = CORRESPONDING zvd_t_hsale( entity MAPPING FROM ENTITY ).

    header-created_on = sy-datum.
    header-entry_time = sy-datum.
    header-name       = sy-uname.

    APPEND header TO gt_hsale.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.
  • Create-By-Association Method
METHOD cba.
  LOOP AT entities_cba INTO DATA(entity_cba).
    LOOP AT entity_cba-%target INTO DATA(target_item).
      DATA(item) = CORRESPONDING zvd_t_isale( target_item MAPPING FROM ENTITY ).

      item-cust_no   = entity_cba-%key-CustNo.
      item-created_on = sy-datum.

      APPEND item TO gt_isale.
    ENDLOOP.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

Result

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Conclusion

By combining Draft Handling, Associations, and Early Numbering, RAP unmanaged scenarios can support complex business objects like Sales Orders, Purchase Orders. This approach ensures flexibility, control, and consistency in transactional applications.

Thank You...

entry.title: Re: Birthday and Work Anniversary Cards on the Home Page
entry.content:
@Annelie  for these type of reminders, they will display for as long as the scheduled jobs are running. The jobs can be cancelled if no longer need to send reminders. 

If you have Employee Central, there are other possible alternatives like sending EC Alert for work anniversary as an Off Cycle Event Batch.  There's a blog post here that explains about the steps on how to achieve it, but this is a custom solution: https://community.sap.com/t5/human-capital-management-blog-posts-by-sap/simple-and-effective-way-of-sending-anniversary-alerts-in-successfactors/ba-p/13398393

3020834 - How To Configure Work Anniversary Alerts
https://me.sap.com/notes/3020834/E

Thanks!

entry.title: Re: Intangible assets
entry.content:
Hello @fendua ,

If I understood your ques correctly, in SAP On-Premise, you cannot define intangible assets as “Items” or “Itemized Services” in the asset master.

✔ Intangible classification is done via Asset Class
✔ “Item vs Service” is controlled in Purchase Order (ME21N) using Item Category

Alternate approach:-

Option 1 – Direct Capitalization (MM → FI-AA)

1️⃣ Create Asset (AS01) or auto-create via PO
2️⃣ PO (ME21N):

Account Assignment = A

Item Category = Standard / D
3️⃣ GR / SES → MIRO
4️⃣ Asset capitalization

Option 2 – AuC + Settlement (Best Practice for Intangibles like Development)

1️⃣ Create AuC (Asset Class: AuC – Intangible)
2️⃣ Procure via:

Service PO (D) OR Material PO

Account Assignment = A (AuC) or WBS
3️⃣ Periodic settlement (KO88 / CJ88)
4️⃣ Final asset capitalization

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: Best Practice for Exporting Large Data Volumes (>1 Mi...
entry.content:
Hello @AliSaad ,

For 1M+ records in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the recommended approach is to use OData APIs with pagination and delta logic, or integrate with SAP BTP tools (Datasphere / Data Intelligence).
UI-based exports and CDS query downloads are not suitable for large-scale extraction and will fail due to system limitations.


If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: MM01 Real time check Classification
entry.content: Did you try in the BAdI implementation to read classification data from current program memory, to use the GET or READ FMs of group CLFM which name end with FROM_BUFFER, for example CLFM_GET_INTERNAL_TABLES.

entry.title: SAP Note 3726893 Impact on SAP Commerce Cloud 2211-jdk21.5
entry.content:
3726893 - [OOTB BUG]@Cacheable SpEL T() static method invocations broken after Spring 6.2 upgrade - SAP for Me states that the cross-site cache pollution occurs with both:

  1. 2211-jdk21.6
  2. 2211-jdk21.7

It does not explicitly state that 2211-jdk21.5 is impacted. 2211-jdk21.5 leverages Spring 6.2.11.

Is 2211-jdk21.5 also impacted by SAP Note 3726893?
